title: AI Benchmarks
lesson:
A benchmark is a fixed test you give to many AI models so you can compare them fairly. It is the AI world's version of a standardised exam. Just as every student in a country sits the same maths paper so their scores can be lined up, a benchmark hands every model the same questions and scores the answers the same way.

Without one, you'd be stuck with vague claims like "this model feels smart" — a benchmark turns that feeling into a number you can argue about. Think of a car magazine's road test: it doesn't just say "nice car," it measures 0-to-60 time, MPG, and braking distance the same way for every car, so you can put a BMW and a Toyota on one chart. Benchmarks do this for models — each test is one lane of the track.

The benchmarks you'll actually hear about

You don't need to memorise these, but knowing the famous ones helps you read announcements:

  • MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding): thousands of multiple-choice questions across 57 subjects — history, law, medicine, maths. A broad "general knowledge" exam.
  • GPQA: graduate-level science questions so hard that even experts with Google struggle. Built because MMLU got too easy.
  • HumanEval and SWE-bench: coding tests. HumanEval asks the model to write small functions; SWE-bench gives it real GitHub bug reports and checks if its fix passes the project's tests. See Coding Models.
  • GSM8K and MATH: maths word problems, popular for testing Reasoning Models.
  • LMArena (the Chatbot Arena): not a fixed quiz at all. Real people chat with two anonymous models, vote which reply they liked better, and the votes build a ranking like a chess Elo ladder. This catches "is it pleasant to use?" — closer to Human Evals.

How scoring works

Most benchmarks report accuracy — the share of questions answered correctly, like 88%. Multiple-choice ones are easy to grade automatically: did the model pick C? Coding ones run the code and check it works (called pass@1: did the first attempt pass?). The grading is just code comparing the model's output to a known right answer — fast, cheap, repeatable.

questions = load("mmlu_test.jsonl")   # each has prompt + correct letter
correct = 0
for q in questions:
    answer = model.generate(q["prompt"])      # ask the model
    if extract_letter(answer) == q["answer"]:  # 'C' == 'C'?
        correct += 1
print(f"accuracy: {correct / len(questions):.1%}")  # e.g. 88.2%

That simplicity is also the danger. The grader only checks the final letter, so how you ask matters enormously. Run the same model with a different prompt or different examples and the score can swing several points.

This is why you'll often see "5-shot" written next to a result: the model was shown 5 example questions first. Comparing a 5-shot score to a 0-shot score is comparing two different exams.

⚠️ Contamination is the biggest trap. Models train on huge scrapes of the internet, and many benchmark questions are on the internet. If the test answers leaked into training data, the model has effectively seen the exam paper beforehand. A sky-high score can mean memorisation, not skill.

Why a high score isn't the whole story

A benchmark measures one narrow lane. A model can ace MMLU and still be slow, expensive, or terrible at your task. Benchmarks ignore cost per token, speed, refusal behaviour, and how it handles your weird company data.

Treat public benchmarks as a first filter — they tell you who's roughly in the running — then run your own small test set on the shortlist. The leaderboard narrows the field; your own eval picks the winner.

There's also Goodhart's Law: "when a measure becomes a target, it stops being a good measure." Once a benchmark gets famous, labs optimise hard for it, sometimes by quietly training on similar data, and scores creep up faster than real ability. That's exactly why hard new benchmarks like GPQA keep appearing — the old ones get "saturated" (everyone scores near the top, so they no longer separate the good from the great). Read benchmark numbers like a thermometer in one room of a house: useful and real, but it doesn't tell you the temperature everywhere.
mentalModel: A benchmark is a standardised exam for AI: every model sits the same paper, graded the same way, so a vague 'feels smart' becomes a number you can compare. But it only tests one lane of the track — high marks on the exam never guarantee a good grade in real life.
takeaways: A benchmark is a standardised exam: same questions, same grading, for every model.
Each benchmark tests one narrow lane — knowledge, code, maths, or human preference.
Watch for contamination, 5-shot vs 0-shot, and saturation; then run your own eval.
mistakes: Trusting one headline number. A single benchmark tests one narrow skill. A model can top MMLU and still flop at your actual task. Always check several benchmarks, then test on your own data.
Ignoring contamination. If a model trained on text that contained the test answers, its score reflects memorisation, not reasoning. Be suspicious of suddenly huge jumps on old, public benchmarks.

Comparing scores measured differently. A 5-shot result (model shown 5 examples first) versus a 0-shot result are different exams. So are different prompts or graders. Only compare like-for-like setups.
Forgetting cost and speed. Benchmarks rank quality only. The 'best' model on a leaderboard may be too slow or too pricey for production. Weigh cost per token and latency alongside accuracy.
Chasing a saturated benchmark. When every top model scores 90%+, the test can no longer tell them apart. Tiny score gaps near the ceiling are noise, not real differences.
Skipping your own eval set. Public benchmarks are a first filter, not the final answer. The cheapest, most honest test is 20–50 examples from your real use case, graded by you.
exercise:
Pick three models you could realistically use (e.g. one large hosted model, one mid-size model, and one small open model you can run locally via Ollama). Find their published scores on two benchmarks — say MMLU (general knowledge) and HumanEval (coding) — and put them in a small table. Then write one sentence per model predicting which real task it would be best for, based on the pattern of scores rather than a single number.

Stretch goal: Build a tiny private benchmark of your own. Write 10 questions from a task you actually care about (e.g. "summarise this support email in one line"), with a clear notion of a good answer. Run all three models on it, grade the answers yourself 1–5, and compare your ranking to the public leaderboards. Note any place where the leaderboard winner loses on your test.
solution: You'll usually find the scores live, not in training: each model card or the lab's announcement lists benchmark numbers, and sites like LMArena aggregate many. The interesting part of the table is the shape: a model that's strong on HumanEval but average on MMLU is a coding specialist; one strong on both is a generalist. For the stretch goal, the lesson lands hardest when your hand-graded ranking disagrees with the public one — for example a small local model beating a 'better' model on your specific summarisation style because it's terser. That gap is the whole point: public benchmarks rank general ability, your eval ranks fitness for your job. When they disagree, trust your own set.
summary: A benchmark is a standardised exam that scores models on a fixed set of questions so you can compare them fairly — MMLU for knowledge, HumanEval/SWE-bench for code, GSM8K/MATH for maths, LMArena for human preference. Most report accuracy, but watch for contamination, mismatched setups (5-shot vs 0-shot), saturation, and Goodhart's Law. Benchmarks ignore cost and speed, so use them as a first filter, then always run your own small eval on your real task before choosing.

diagram.caption: Every model takes the same fixed test, an auto-grader turns answers into comparable accuracy scores, but the number ignores cost, speed, contamination, and your real task.

quiz.q: Why is a benchmark useful compared to just saying a model 'feels smart'?
options: It gives every model the same test, scored the same way, so results can be compared fairly
It guarantees the model will be good at your specific product task
It measures how fast and cheap the model is to run
It proves the model has never seen the questions before
quiz.explain: A benchmark's value is standardisation: same questions, same grading, so a vague feeling becomes a comparable number. It does not promise fitness for your task, nor measure cost/speed.

quiz.q: A model's MMLU score jumps from 71% to 96% right after a benchmark dataset became widely shared online. What should you suspect first?
options: The model suddenly became far more intelligent
Contamination — the test answers may have leaked into its training data
The grader switched from accuracy to Elo
quiz.explain: A huge jump on an old, public benchmark right after it spread online is a classic sign of contamination: the model effectively studied the exam paper, so the score reflects memorisation, not skill.

quiz.q: You see Model A scored 88% (5-shot) and Model B scored 84% (0-shot) on the same benchmark. What is the safest conclusion?
options: Model A is clearly better by 4 points
Model B is better because 0-shot is harder
You can't compare them directly — different shot counts are effectively different exams
Both scores are invalid because shots don't matter
quiz.explain: 5-shot shows the model 5 examples first while 0-shot shows none, so the two setups are different tests. You can only compare like-for-like; the 4-point gap here is meaningless.

quiz.q: Two top models both score ~95% on a benchmark, with a 0.3-point difference. By Goodhart's Law and saturation, what's the wise read?
options: The 0.3-point winner is the better model for everything
The benchmark is saturated; tiny gaps are noise, so use a harder test or your own eval
You should pick whichever model is more famous
Saturation means both models are unreliable and should be avoided
quiz.explain: Near the ceiling a benchmark can no longer separate models, and heavy optimisation toward a famous target (Goodhart's Law) erodes its meaning. Treat tiny gaps as noise and judge with a harder or task-specific eval.

keyTerms.def: A fixed set of questions or tasks given to many models and scored the same way, so their abilities can be compared fairly — like a standardised exam.
The share of benchmark questions a model answered correctly, often shown as a percentage like 88%.
A broad multiple-choice benchmark covering 57 subjects (history, law, medicine, maths) used to test general knowledge.
Coding benchmarks. HumanEval grades small functions; SWE-bench gives real GitHub bug reports and checks if the model's fix passes the project's tests.
Showing the model k example questions before the real one (e.g. '5-shot'). It changes scores, so only compare results with the same number of examples.
When benchmark questions or answers leaked into a model's training data, so a high score reflects memorising the test rather than real skill.
When most top models score near the maximum on a benchmark, so it can no longer tell them apart and a harder one is needed.
'When a measure becomes a target, it stops being a good measure.' Once labs optimise for a famous benchmark, its scores stop tracking real ability.
A ranking from head-to-head human votes (like chess ratings). LMArena lets real users pick the better of two anonymous replies to build a leaderboard.
